region_of_origin: China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried beancurd skin; SNOMED CT coverage of processed soy products is limited.

serving.description: 1 serving (approx. 30 g dry weight, rehydrated before cooking)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried beancurd skin is approximately 30 g dry weight, which rehydrates to roughly 90–100 g and is commonly used in soups, braises, or stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: Dried beancurd skin (Fuchok) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried beancurd skin (Fuchok), CR ID F09040002. All core macros and micronutrients reflect HPB-reported values at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried beancurd skin (fuchok) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the closest analogous food: tofu / soy protein products, which consistently register GI 15 (low) in peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Carbohydrate content is very low at 6.04 g/100g; glycemic load per 30 g serving = (15 × 6.04 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.27, rounded to 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (92 mg/100g) with high potassium (430 mg/100g) supporting DASH-aligned vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Dried beancurd skin is suitable for hypertensive patients in its plain dried form; sodium is well below the 200 mg/100g caution threshold, and the high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports blood pressure regulation, but sodium load increases substantially when prepared in salty broths or sauces, so low-sodium cooking liquids should be requested.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (6.04 g/100g) and very high protein (53.85 g/100g) producing minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried beancurd skin has a very low estimated GI of 15 and a glycaemic load of only 0.3 per 30 g serving, making it an excellent protein source for type 2 diabetic patients; pairing with high-GI sauces or sweet braising liquids should be avoided to preserve the low overall glycaemic load of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (30.3 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~4.4 g/100g; soy isoflavones provide cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While total fat is high at 30.3 g/100g, saturated fat is estimated at approximately 4.4 g/100g (below the 5 g threshold), and soy isoflavones in beancurd skin are associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; cardiovascular patients should consume moderate portions and avoid deep-frying preparation, which would increase saturated fat and AGE load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, absent trans fat, soy isoflavones with mild anti-inflammatory properties, and calcium supporting bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried beancurd skin is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients when prepared without deep-frying; calcium at 159 mg/100g supports bone health relevant to patients on corticosteroid therapy, and soy isoflavones provide mild anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried beancurd skin, known in Cantonese as Fuchok (腐竹) and in Mandarin as Fu Zhu, is a traditional Chinese soy product made by lifting and drying the protein-lipid film that forms on the surface of heated soy milk. The resulting product is a light yellow, thin, brittle sheet with a mild, slightly nutty soy flavour. It is one of the most protein-dense plant foods available, providing 53.85 g of protein per 100 g dry weight, making it nutritionally comparable to lean meat. Total fat is 30.3 g/100g, with estimated saturated fat of approximately 4.4 g/100g; carbohydrate is very low at 6.04 g/100g. Sodium is low at 92 mg/100g and potassium is high at 430 mg/100g, making it DASH-diet compatible in its plain form. Calcium at 159 mg/100g and iron at 5.67 mg/100g are notable for a plant-based food. Before cooking, dried beancurd skin is typically soaked in water to rehydrate, after which it becomes soft and silky. It is widely used in Chinese cuisine in soups, braised dishes, stir-fries, and as a wrapping for dim sum fillings. In Singapore, it appears in dessert soups (tong sui), savoury braises, and as a topping in congee. Clinically, it is suitable for type 2 diabetic patients due to its very low glycaemic index (estimated GI 15) and minimal glycaemic load. It is appropriate for hypertensive patients in plain preparation. Cardiovascular patients should moderate portion size due to total fat content. It is vegan, gluten-free, and halal.

What is fu zhu made of?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F09040002) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, dietary fibre, and sugar are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for dried tofu skin (closest approved-source equivalent) as HPB does not publish full fatty acid profiles. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dried beancurd skin is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tofu skin as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (4.4 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Tofu, dried-frozen (koyadofu)' and cross-referenced with USDA entry 16120 'Soybeans, mature seeds, dry roasted' as the closest approved-source analogue; this is consistent with the health_suitability estimate in the input record. 3. dietary_fibre_g (0.4 g) and sugar_g (0.0 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried tofu skin; HPB does not publish these values for this item. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dried beancurd skin not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this specific product. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this item and are not available in a directly matching USDA SR Legacy entry as of 2026-04-16. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on tofu/soy protein products in Atkinson FS et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; no direct GI measurement for dried beancurd skin exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302757 (tofu skin food product) is the closest defensible FoodOn match; FoodEx2 code A00LC (Soya bean curd skin, dried) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. These codes should be verified against the live ontology databases before production import.
